title: Remove regfile reset fanout
motivation:
The current register file resets all 32 architectural registers with a
synchronous for-loop. On FPGA this discourages LUTRAM-style inference for the
32x32 storage and adds reset muxing/fanout across a hot, frequently accessed
structure. RV32I does not require integer registers other than x0 to have a
defined reset value, and x0 is already hardwired through read/write gating.

defined reset value, and x0 is already hardwired through read/write gating.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Change rtl/reg_file.sv so reset no longer clears the backing register array.
Preserve the existing write-first bypass, x0 read-as-zero behavior, and write
suppression to x0. This should reduce flip-flop/reset routing pressure and may
let synthesis map the register file to distributed RAM or a smaller muxed
structure, improving Fmax with negligible CoreMark CPI risk because compiled C
code initializes live registers before use.

title: Resolve branches in ID
motivation:
The current five-stage core resolves every branch and jump in EX, so each
taken redirect flushes two younger instructions. CoreMark spends many cycles
in tight counted loops and function-call-heavy list/state code, making taken
control transfers a direct CPI cost. Reducing the redirect penalty by one
stage should improve iterations per cycle without changing the nret=1 RVFI
contract.

stage should improve iterations per cycle without changing the nret=1 RVFI

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add an ID-stage early redirect path for conditional branches, JAL, and JALR
when their operands are already available from the regfile or safe same-cycle
bypasses. The ID stage can compute immediates, branch comparisons, and jump
targets one cycle earlier than EX; when it redirects, IF emits a bubble and
ID/EX captures a bubble so the control-transfer instruction still retires
through the normal pipeline with correct RVFI pc_wdata. Cases needing an
unavailable producer, especially load-use and EX/MEM load results, fall back
to the existing EX resolution path for correctness.

title: Add tiny BTB and BHT branch predictor
motivation: The current five-stage pipeline always fetches pc+4 and resolves all branches and jumps in EX, so every taken control-flow instruction pays a two-instruction wrong-path flush. CoreMark contains tight loops and repeated call/return style control flow where even a very small FPGA-friendly predictor should learn the hot backedges and reduce bubbles. A predictor is especially attractive under the random instruction-stall workload because it improves useful fetch placement without adding memory-system complexity.

A predictor is especially attractive under the random instruction-stall workload because it improves useful fetch placement without adding memory-system complexity.
hypothesis.hypothesis: Add a small direct-mapped branch target buffer with 2-bit saturating direction counters in the IF stage. The IF stage predicts taken when the PC tag hits and the BHT counter is weak/strong taken, uses the cached target as next_pc, and carries predicted_pc/predicted_taken metadata down the pipeline. EX compares the actual resolved next PC against the prediction, redirects only on mispredict, and updates the BTB/BHT for branches and jumps. This should preserve correctness through the existing redirect/flush machinery while converting most steady-state CoreMark loop backedges from taken-branch flushes into correctly predicted fetches.

title: Qualify load-use source checks
motivation:
The current load-use hazard detector compares a load destination against both
raw source bitfields of the following instruction for every opcode. For many
RV32I instructions, one or both of those bitfields are immediate bits or are
otherwise unused, so a coincidental match with the prior load's rd injects a
needless one-cycle bubble. CoreMark has frequent loads followed by immediate
arithmetic, address updates, jumps, and upper-immediate instructions where
avoiding false dependencies should improve iterations per cycle without
touching the proven datapath or memory protocol.

hypothesis.motivation:

avoiding false dependencies should improve iterations per cycle without

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Teach the hazard unit which IF/ID operands are actually consumed before
applying the load-use comparison. Keep the true load-use stall for branches,
stores, R-type operations, JALR, OP-IMM rs1 uses, and real load base-register
dependencies, but ignore rs2 for I-type/load/JALR immediates and ignore both
operands for LUI, AUIPC, JAL, FENCE, and EBREAK. This is a small combinational
decode in the existing hazard path that should remove spurious bubbles while
preserving all forwarding, RVFI order, trap, and bus-stall behavior.

title: Move M extension to multicycle unit
motivation:
The current EX stage instantiates three 64-bit multipliers plus divider and
remainder operators inside the always-active combinational ALU. Even when
CoreMark is executing ordinary integer, branch, and address-generation ops,
synthesis must carry that large structural cone through the ALU module and
EX-stage muxing, which is a likely Fmax limiter on the Tang Nano 20K.

CoreMark's hot loops use mostly RV32I control, load/store, and simple ALU
operations, so paying extra cycles only on rare RV32M instructions should be
a favorable trade if the common EX path becomes smaller and faster.

hypothesis.motivation:

CoreMark is executing ordinary integer, branch, and address-generation ops,

CoreMark's hot loops use mostly RV32I control, load/store, and simple ALU
operations, so paying extra cycles only on rare RV32M instructions should be
a favorable trade if the common EX path becomes smaller and faster.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Split RV32M operations out of the combinational ALU into a dedicated
multicycle mdu module, leaving alu.sv as a compact single-cycle RV32I datapath
for add/sub/logic/compare/shift/LUI/address generation. The EX stage detects
ALU_MUL* and ALU_DIV/REM* operations, launches the MDU, and stalls the front
half of the pipeline until the result is available, then writes the result
into the normal EX/MEM register so RVFI ordering and single-retire semantics
stay unchanged.

This structural decoupling should raise Fmax by removing the multiplier and
divider hardware from the common EX critical path. The CPI loss should be
limited because CoreMark's counted loops are not dominated by M-extension
arithmetic, while every non-M instruction benefits from the shorter path.

title: Static backward branch predictor
motivation:
The current five-stage core always fetches pc+4 and pays a two-instruction
redirect penalty for every taken branch and jump resolved in EX. CoreMark has
many small counted loops where backward conditional branches are taken most
iterations, plus direct JAL call/loop control transfers with targets fully
encoded in the fetched instruction. A tiny static predictor can recover much
of that CPI loss with less state and less correctness risk than the previous
BTB/BHT attempt.

encoded in the fetched instruction. A tiny static predictor can recover much
of that CPI loss with less state and less correctness risk than the previous
BTB/BHT attempt.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add decode-light prediction in IF for direct control flow only: predict JAL
taken to its J-immediate target, predict conditional branches with negative
B-immediates taken, and leave forward branches plus JALR as pc+4. Carry the
predicted next PC with the instruction through IF/ID and ID/EX. EX still
computes the authoritative next PC and RVFI pc_wdata, but redirects and
flushes only when the predicted next PC differs from the actual next PC.
This keeps the existing retirement semantics intact while eliminating the
steady-state flush on hot backward CoreMark loop branches and direct jumps.

title: Add one-entry fetch skid buffer
motivation:
The current front end presents imem_data directly into decode and only holds
the PC when the back half of the pipeline stalls. Under the benchmark's
random instruction stalls plus occasional load-use, dmem, and MDU stalls,
this tightly couples fetch availability to decode acceptance and can waste
cycles where the instruction bus is ready but ID/EX cannot yet consume the
fetched word.

Prior structural attempts at earlier branch resolution and predictor-like
front-end changes regressed or broke because they added control-path work on
the fetch next-PC path. A tiny skid buffer is a narrower structural change:
it should improve useful instruction delivery during stalls while preserving
the existing EX-stage authoritative redirect and RVFI pc_wdata semantics.

high but decode is stalled by load-use, dmem backpressure, or an MDU busy
period, capture the current fetched instruction and PC instead of emitting a
bubble or requiring the instruction bus to be ready again later. When decode
becomes available, drain the buffered instruction before advancing to newer
fetch data.

Redirects must invalidate the buffer and retain the existing priority where
the EX redirect updates the PC even during stalls. The buffer should not try
to predict branches or move resolution earlier; it only decouples fetch from
decode acceptance. This should raise CoreMark iterations/cycle in the random
iStall workload with modest LUT/FF cost and lower correctness risk than
branch-control restructuring.

title: Tiny return address predictor
motivation:
The previous predictor attempts show that putting branch-immediate decode or
BTB/BHT lookup directly on the IF next-PC path can either break RVFI redirect
semantics or lose enough Fmax to erase loop CPI gains. CoreMark still has many
call and return transfers, and returns are expensive in the current core
because every JALR resolves in EX and flushes two younger instructions.

A very small return-address predictor targets only the indirect-control case
that static backward prediction cannot cover, while avoiding a broad branch
direction predictor in the fetch critical path.

should keep the normal pc+4 path for all other instructions, so the added

prediction or decode-heavy backward prediction.

motivation:
The current best bench core is already dominated by FPGA Fmax rather than
large CPI opportunities, and the recent successful micro-opts both came
from removing unnecessary reset or pipeline metadata. One remaining control
bit, mem_to_reg, is redundant with mem_read: every legal load asserts both,
and no non-load instruction should select the loaded-data path.

title: Register the IF ID boundary
motivation:
The current front end has no real IF/ID pipeline register: imem_data, PC,
decode, immediate generation, register-file address selection, and the ID/EX
capture all sit in one cycle. After the successful MDU split and metadata
pruning, the remaining Tang Nano 20K fitness is likely limited by routing and
long stage boundaries rather than by CoreMark CPI alone.

Prior structural and predictor attempts that added front-end speculation or
replay state regressed because they put new work on the fetch next-PC path.
A plain IF/ID register is a narrower structural retiming that does not try to
predict control flow; it simply cuts the fetch-to-decode path and lets the PC
and instruction memory interface settle before decode consumes the word.

branch-immediate decode to the fetch next-PC path. The CPI cost should be
limited to pipeline fill and the existing redirect flush semantics; CoreMark's
bracketed region is long enough that a modest Fmax gain should dominate.

motivation: Prior predictor attempts show that broad IF-stage decode, BTB/BHT state, and return-shape logic can cost more Tang Nano 20K Fmax than they recover in CPI. motivation:
The best prior bench improvements came from removing reset fanout and dead
pipeline metadata, while recent front-end and predictor changes lost Fmax.
The current RTL still carries the full decoded ctrl_t through EX/MEM and
MEM/WB even though most of those bits are only needed in EX. On a small FPGA
this wastes flip-flops, mux inputs, and routing on every pipeline stage.

title: Add posted store buffer
motivation:
The current pipeline freezes the entire front half whenever a store sits in
EX/MEM and io_dmemReady is low. In the benchmark's random dmem-stall mode,
that means a store with no register result can block unrelated following ALU
work even though in-order retirement only requires the store request to be
preserved and later memory operations to remain ordered.

Prior structural attempts that touched fetch replay or prediction regressed,
while the successful changes reduced hot-path pressure without changing the
front-end next-PC path. A tiny posted store buffer is a back-end structural
decoupling point: it targets random dmem backpressure and CoreMark's store
traffic without adding IF critical-path logic.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a one-entry posted store buffer at the MEM stage. When a valid store
reaches EX/MEM while io_dmemReady is low and the buffer is empty, capture its
address, replicated write data, byte mask, and RVFI sideband, let the store
retire exactly once through the normal channel-0 path, and allow subsequent
non-memory instructions to keep flowing. The buffer continues driving the
dmem write request until io_dmemReady accepts it.

To preserve precise in-order memory behavior, stall any later load or store
while the buffer is occupied, and keep the existing full-pipeline dmem stall
behavior when a load misses readiness or when a second store arrives before
the buffer drains. This should recover cycles otherwise lost to isolated
store backpressure under the apples-to-apples random stall model, with a
smaller Fmax risk than fetch-side structural changes because the added state
sits behind EX/MEM and does not affect branch redirect or PC selection.

title: ID-stage learned target predictor
motivation:
Prior predictor attempts for this core regressed because opcode/immediate decode,
BTB comparisons, return-shape checks, and predicted-next-PC muxing were placed on
the IF next-PC path. CoreMark still pays a two-instruction penalty on recurring
taken loop branches and direct jumps, so the remaining promising predictor angle
is to avoid the IF critical path while still reducing taken-control bubbles.

A predictor consulted in ID can use the already-fetched PC/instruction timing
point and redirect one cycle earlier than the current EX resolver. It only saves
one of the two wrong-path slots, but it should cost less Fmax than full IF
speculation and does not require early operand compare or immediate generation.

taken-branch bubbles.

title: Reset only pipeline valid bits
motivation:
The current best core is Fmax-sensitive and the strongest micro-opt wins came
from removing unnecessary reset fanout and dead pipeline metadata. The ID/EX,

including RVFI data fields, operand values, immediates, memory data, and PC
metadata that are ignored whenever valid is low. On the Tang Nano 20K this

architectural behavior.

boundaries without changing CPI, RVFI order, memory ordering, or the nret=1
top-level interface. It follows the proven regfile-reset lesson while avoiding
prior failed control-bundle repacking and operand-source changes.

title: Deepen posted store buffer
motivation:
The only recent structural improvement came from adding a one-entry posted store buffer, which lets an isolated store retire while its dmem write drains under random backpressure. That design still blocks when a second store arrives before the first buffered write is accepted, so short CoreMark store bursts can collapse back into full-pipeline stalls.

Front-end structural changes have repeatedly regressed by adding fetch-path or replay complexity. A slightly deeper store queue stays in the proven back-end decoupling direction and avoids changing branch, fetch, or RVFI top-level semantics.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Replace the single posted-store entry in mem_stage with a two-entry ordered store buffer. When a valid non-misaligned store reaches MEM and dmem is not ready, enqueue it if either slot is free and still let that store retire exactly once through channel 0; the oldest buffered entry continues to drive the dmem write port until accepted. Younger loads and stores must stall behind any buffered store to preserve precise memory ordering, but younger non-memory instructions can continue as they do today.

This should improve CoreMark iterations/cycle in the random dmem-stall regime by absorbing back-to-back stores and store bursts, while adding only a small amount of MEM-stage control that is off the fragile IF next-PC path. RVFI mem_wmask/mem_wdata for the retiring store remain sourced from the current EX/MEM instruction, so order, trap, and single-retire invariants stay unchanged.

title: Not-taken branch confidence predictor
motivation:
Prior predictor attempts for this core regressed because target prediction put
tag checks, immediate decode, next-PC muxing, and speculation metadata on the
fragile front-end path. The current core already fetches pc+4, which is the
correct path for not-taken conditional branches; the remaining predictor
opportunity is to make use of that cheap default without adding a target mux.

CoreMark contains a mix of loop backedges and forward conditional exits. A
small direction-only confidence table can learn branches that are almost
always not taken and let EX avoid asserting redirect/flush work for those
correctly predicted fall-through cases, while taken branches and jumps keep
using the existing authoritative EX redirect path.

Add a tiny direct-mapped direction predictor that stores only conditional
branch PCs and 2-bit not-taken/taken confidence counters. IF still always
presents pc+4 as the next fetch address; there is no predicted target path,
no branch immediate decode in IF, and no speculative target metadata. ID only
carries a single predicted_not_taken bit for conditional branches based on a
PC tag hit and a not-taken counter state.

EX remains the authoritative resolver and updates the table after each valid
conditional branch. For predicted-not-taken branches that are actually not
taken, behavior is identical to the current pc+4 flow. For taken branches, EX
uses the existing redirect/flush path and trains the counter toward taken.

should have much lower Fmax risk because the fetch next-PC cone is unchanged
and the extra pipeline metadata is one bit.

title: Shorten MDU multiply latency
motivation:
The current best core already moved RV32M operations out of the common ALU path,
which improved Fmax substantially, but the MDU still assigns all multiply-family
operations a two-cycle internal latency even though the product is computed and
captured at launch. CoreMark uses some RV32M multiplies in its matrix/state code,
so each avoidable MDU hold directly reduces counted cycles while leaving the hot
RV32I EX path and front-end timing unchanged.

while keeping DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU at their existing long latency. The multiplier
result is already formed combinationally before result_q is captured, so this is
a control-latency reduction rather than adding new arithmetic hardware. It should
improve CoreMark iterations/cycle on multiply instructions with little Fmax risk,
and it does not affect memory ordering, fetch behavior, RVFI order, or nret=1 IO.

title: Bypass ready load-use pairs
motivation:
The current five-stage pipeline still pays a fixed one-cycle bubble whenever
a load in EX is followed by an instruction that consumes its rd, even when
the data-memory port is ready the next cycle. CoreMark has many pointer and
array dependent load-use pairs, and the benchmark's random dmem stalls already
provide the necessary full-pipeline hold when the load data is not available.

Prior structural wins came from back-end decoupling and keeping the fragile
fetch path unchanged. A MEM-to-EX load-result bypass targets a direct CPI
loss in the back half of the pipeline without adding branch prediction,
replay buffering, or deeper store-queue placement pressure.

Add a dedicated load-result forwarding path from mem_stage into ex_stage.

already computes the sign/zero-extended load_data combinationally; expose
that value plus a valid/rd indication and teach forward_unit/ex_stage to use
it for the immediately following consumer instead of forwarding the load's
address-valued alu_result. Change the load-use hazard so it stalls only when
the producer load cannot deliver data, such as during dmem backpressure or
when an older posted store forces memory ordering.

memory ordering because the dependent instruction still retires after the
load and records the actual forwarded rs values. It should improve
iterations/cycle on CoreMark's dependent load chains; the main risk is a
modest Fmax hit from the new MEM-to-EX bypass mux, so the expected gain is

title: ID-stage JAL target predictor
motivation:
Every prior predictor attempt for this core regressed because it put too much
work on the fragile fetch next-PC path or carried broad prediction metadata
through the pipeline. CoreMark still contains direct calls and direct jumps
that are always taken, and those currently wait until EX before redirecting,
paying two wrong-path slots each time. A predictor slot should therefore focus
on the smallest control-flow case with a guaranteed direction and encoded
target.

title: Iterative divider MDU
motivation:
The current MDU moved RV32M work out of the common ALU path, but the real
hardware branch still uses SystemVerilog divide and remainder operators to
compute DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU combinationally at start. Even with a 34-cycle
latency counter, synthesis must build large divider/modulo cones that can
consume LUTs and constrain placement on Tang Nano 20K. CoreMark's timed hot
path is dominated by RV32I plus occasional multiplies, so rare division
latency is a better tradeoff than carrying a wide combinational divider.

small iterative restoring divider that reuses one subtract/compare step per
cycle and produces both quotient and remainder after roughly 32 cycles. Keep
the existing interface, ALTOPS formulas, multiply results, divide-by-zero,
signed overflow, and RV32M result semantics unchanged. This should reduce
divider LUT/routing pressure and may improve median Fmax while leaving the
common RV32I pipeline, memory protocol, RVFI retirement, and nret=1 IO
untouched. The CPI risk is limited because CoreMark rarely executes integer
division in the measured loop, and the old advertised divide latency was
already multicycle.

title: Precompute forwarding selects in ID
motivation:
The current EX stage resolves both operand-forwarding mux selects with two
5-bit destination comparisons against EX/MEM and MEM/WB immediately before
the ALU, branch comparator, MDU launch, and EX/MEM register boundary. After
the successful MDU split, Tang Nano 20K fitness appears dominated by hot
RV32I timing and routing rather than rare long-latency M operations.

Prior structural wins came from removing common-path pressure, while attempts
to add front-end prediction, replay, or deeper queues regressed. Moving the
forwarding compare work one stage earlier is a structural retiming that keeps
fetch, branch resolution, memory ordering, and the nret=1 RVFI contract
unchanged.

and the instruction currently in MEM. Latch those two 2-bit selects into the
ID/EX payload, then make ex_stage use the precomputed selects directly for
its rs1/rs2 muxes.

immediately ahead of a consumer still bubbles rather than forwarding the
address-valued EX/MEM result. RVFI source reporting remains based on the

title: Single backedge loop predictor
motivation:
Prior predictor attempts for this core lost fitness because they added IF
decode, multi-entry tag/counter tables, return-stack checks, extra redirect
sources, or broad predicted-next-PC metadata to a timing-sensitive front end.
CoreMark still pays the full EX redirect penalty on hot counted-loop
backedges, so the best predictor angle is a deliberately tiny loop-only
target predictor that learns one recurring taken backward branch without
touching direct jumps, returns, or forward branches.

title: Decode explicit MDU op bit
motivation:
Recent bench results show the current core is more sensitive to Tang Nano 20K
Fmax and routing than to speculative CPI tweaks. The EX stage still detects
RV32M operations by comparing the 5-bit ALU opcode against all eight MDU
encodings, then uses that result to drive the MDU start, EX busy/stall, result
mux, and RVFI operand capture paths. That decode sits in the hot execute
control cone even though the decoder already knows exactly when funct7=1 marks
an M-extension instruction.

title: Register final writeback data
motivation:
The current MEM/WB boundary carries both alu_result and read_data plus a
mem_to_reg control bit, then wb_stage selects the final value on the same
path that drives regfile write data, MEM/WB forwarding, and RVFI rd_wdata.
Prior successful structural work came from removing common-path pressure and
pipeline metadata, while front-end replay/prediction changes and deeper
queues have repeatedly regressed on Tang Nano 20K.

A narrow back-end retiming can reduce late writeback muxing and MEM/WB
routing without touching fetch, branch resolution, memory ordering, the
posted store buffer depth, or the nret=1 RVFI contract.

path that drives regfile write data, MEM/WB forwarding, and RVFI rd_wdata.

already sign/zero-extended load_data, while all other register-writing
instructions write the EX/MEM alu_result. Then make wb_stage and the RVFI
rd_wdata path consume the registered wb_data directly, and remove the
late mem_to_reg read_data/alu_result mux from the writeback/forwarding path.

Unlike the earlier mem_to_reg pruning attempt, this does not make mem_read
drive a wider fanout cone. It preserves the existing load extraction logic
and simply registers the selected result at the stage boundary, reducing
MEM/WB payload width and simplifying the high-fanout WB data source used by
the regfile and EX forwarding.

title: Stall-shadow branch predictor
motivation:
Prior predictor attempts on this core consistently lost fitness when they put
target lookup, immediate decode, extra PC muxing, or broad prediction metadata
on the normal no-stall fetch path. CoreMark still pays taken-control penalties,
but the benchmark also injects random instruction and data stalls that leave
the front end holding a fetched instruction for one or more cycles. Those held
cycles are an opportunity to prepare a redirect without lengthening the common
pc+4 fetch path.

blocked by imem backpressure, dmem backpressure, load-use, or MDU busy. During

their static target off the normal PC-update critical path. When the stall
releases, IF may use this precomputed target for a one-cycle early redirect;

predicted target to suppress the duplicate redirect only on an exact match or
repair through the existing redirect/flush path on mismatch.

and prediction on uninterrupted fetch cycles. It should recover some control
penalty after random stall windows in CoreMark while leaving the proven normal
fetch path, memory ordering, RVFI order, and nret=1 top-level interface intact.

title: Split writeback commit sideband
motivation:
The latest structural improvement came from adding narrow forwarding sidebands so EX no longer depends directly on wide RVFI-heavy pipeline bundles. A similar fanout issue remains at the final writeback boundary: the full MEM/WB record still feeds regfile writeback, write-first register-file bypass, forwarding sideband generation, and RVFI reporting.

The MEM/WB record is intentionally wide because it carries PC, operand, memory, trap, and instruction metadata for RVFI. Those fields are cold for the hot regfile/writeback path, so keeping the commit datapath tied to the same packed struct can cost routing and mux delay on Tang Nano 20K without improving CPI.

title: Tiny learned JAL predictor
motivation:
The predictor history for this core shows that branch-direction tables, loop
target caches, return stacks, and static immediate decoding usually lose more
Tang Nano 20K Fmax than they recover in CoreMark CPI. The remaining useful
predictor target should therefore avoid conditional branches, JALR returns,
IF immediate decoding, and wide predicted-next-PC metadata.

Direct JAL call and jump sites are always taken and have stable targets in
CoreMark. Learning only the most recent direct JAL target gives a predictor
that can recover recurring call/jump flushes with a single PC compare and a
registered target, rather than a general branch predictor.

cheaper than prior predictors because the target is learned from EX rather
than decoded in IF, and the metadata is narrower than a full predicted target.

title: Redirect target prefetch buffer
motivation:
Prior predictor attempts for this core have repeatedly regressed because they
put target lookup, immediate decode, direction state, extra redirect sources,
or prediction metadata on timing-sensitive fetch and execute paths. CoreMark
still loses cycles after taken branches and jumps, especially when the random
instruction-stall model withholds the redirected target fetch after EX has
already identified the correct PC.

A narrower predictor-category opportunity is to avoid guessing direction or
target at all: use the authoritative EX redirect target as soon as it is
known to prefetch and optionally buffer the first target instruction. This
targets post-redirect iStall bubbles while keeping branch correctness,
RVFI pc_wdata, and mispredict recovery identical to the current pipeline.

Add a one-entry redirect target prefetch buffer in the front end. On a valid
EX redirect, if the instruction bus is ready, temporarily drive the imem
address with the redirect target and capture the returned target instruction
plus its PC into a small buffer while the normal IF output remains flushed.
On the next cycle, when the PC has been updated to that target, the IF stage
can supply the buffered instruction even if io_imemReady is low, and the
hazard unit should treat that buffered hit as effective instruction readiness.

This deliberately avoids BTB/BHT tables, branch-immediate decode in IF,
learned target state, return stacks, predicted-next-PC metadata, and EX
prediction validation. It should improve iterations/cycle only in cycles
where a real redirect is followed by random imem backpressure, with a smaller
Fmax risk than prior predictors because the correctness path remains the
existing authoritative EX redirect and channel-0 RVFI retirement flow.

title: Store buffer drain refill
motivation:
The only durable CPI-oriented structural improvement in this core has been
the one-entry posted store buffer. It lets an isolated store retire while a
stalled dmem write drains, but it still creates a full bubble when the buffer
becomes ready to drain in the same cycle that the next store is waiting in
EX/MEM. Deepening the buffer to two entries failed placement, so the next
promising structural angle is to improve throughput of the existing single
entry rather than add queue depth or front-end speculation.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Teach the one-entry posted store buffer to perform a same-cycle drain/refill:
when an older buffered store owns the dmem port and io_dmemReady is high,
allow a younger valid aligned store already in EX/MEM to retire and replace
the buffer entry, while the old entry is the one driven to memory that cycle.
Younger loads and non-store memory operations should still stall behind any
buffered store because the single dmem port cannot service them during the
drain cycle, and non-memory instructions keep the existing behavior.

This keeps the proven one-entry structure and precise store ordering, but
removes a recurrent bubble for store bursts under random dmem backpressure.
It avoids the failed two-entry FIFO data movement and does not touch fetch,
branch prediction, MDU behavior, RVFI ordering, or the nret=1 top-level IO.

motivation:

hypothesis.motivation:
Prior predictor attempts on this core repeatedly lost fitness because they
added same-cycle IF decode, table reads, target muxing, return-stack logic,
or wide predicted-next-PC metadata to timing-sensitive fetch and execute
paths. The remaining control-flow opportunity is to reduce taken-loop flushes
only when the predictor decision can be prepared one cycle early and consumed
as a tiny registered hint, rather than as a fresh lookup in the PC update cone.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a one-entry registered next-PC hint predictor for learned taken
conditional backedges. When EX resolves a valid taken branch whose target is
below its PC, record the branch PC, target, and a one-bit generation tag. While
IF is fetching the previous sequential PC, compare pc+4 against the recorded
branch PC and register a hint for the following cycle; if the next PC reaches
that hinted branch, the PC update mux consumes only the registered hint target.

Carry only a predicted_hint bit and generation tag with the instruction so EX
can suppress the duplicate redirect only when the resolved branch still
matches the captured entry; otherwise the existing redirect/flush path repairs
execution. This intentionally avoids BTB/BHT arrays, IF immediate decode,
JAL/JALR prediction, and full predicted-target metadata, aiming for a small
CPI gain on CoreMark loops with less Fmax risk than prior predictor variants.

changes.description: Add minimal predicted-hint and generation metadata to IF/ID and ID/EX bundles, avoiding full predicted target fields.

Validate hinted branches against the captured generation and resolved target, suppressing duplicate redirects only on exact matches.
Wire predictor update and validation signals between IF and EX without changing the nret=1 top-level RVFI interface.
